title: Managing AI Projects
description: Learn to plan, scope, and execute AI projects — from setting realistic timelines to managing the unique risks and iteration cycles of AI development.
learningObjectives: Plan AI projects with realistic timelines that account for the unique uncertainty, iteration cycles, and evaluation challenges of AI development
Identify and manage the risks specific to AI initiatives — from data quality issues to model behavior drift to stakeholder expectation misalignment
Coordinate cross-functional teams across engineering, data science, design, and legal to deliver AI features effectively

sections.title: AI Project Planning: How It Differs
sections.content:
AI project planning breaks many of the assumptions that work for traditional software development. Requirements are fuzzier, timelines are less predictable, and "done" is a moving target. Understanding these differences is the first step to managing AI projects effectively.

**Why Traditional Planning Fails for AI**

Traditional software features have clear acceptance criteria: the button does X when clicked, the API returns Y given input Z. AI features have probabilistic acceptance criteria: the model produces a good response most of the time, for most inputs, according to most evaluators. This fundamental shift affects every aspect of planning.

• **Scope is harder to define.** "Summarize customer emails" sounds simple, but the complexity depends on email length, language, formality, domain jargon, and thread depth. You won't know the true scope until you've tested with real data.
• **Estimation is less reliable.** An engineer can estimate how long it takes to build a REST endpoint. Estimating how long it takes to get Claude to reliably extract structured data from unstructured text is more like estimating how long it takes to solve a puzzle you've never seen.
• **"Done" is a spectrum.** A traditional feature ships and works. An AI feature ships at 70% quality and improves to 85% over weeks of prompt iteration. Your project plan needs to account for both the initial launch and the improvement trajectory.

**The Phased Planning Approach**

Instead of a single project plan with a single launch date, structure AI projects in phases:

• **Phase 1: Feasibility (1-2 weeks)** — Can Claude do this task at all? Build a quick proof-of-concept with real data. Test the hardest cases first, not the easiest. The goal is to determine whether the feature is viable, not to build it.
• **Phase 2: Prompt Development (1-3 weeks)** — Iterate on prompts, system messages, and output formats. Establish quality baselines. This phase is where most of the AI-specific work happens, and it's the hardest to estimate.
• **Phase 3: Integration (1-2 weeks)** — Connect the Claude integration to your product's UI, data pipeline, and error handling. This phase is more predictable because it's traditional software engineering.
• **Phase 4: Evaluation and Hardening (1-2 weeks)** — Systematic testing across edge cases, performance optimization, guardrail implementation. Don't skip this phase to hit a deadline.
• **Phase 5: Launch and Iteration (ongoing)** — Ship to users and begin the continuous improvement cycle. Plan for at least 4 weeks of post-launch prompt iteration in your roadmap.

**Estimation Heuristics**

Since traditional estimation methods are less reliable, use these AI-specific heuristics:

• **Double your initial estimate for prompt development.** If an engineer thinks they can get the prompts working in one week, budget two. The last 20% of quality takes 80% of the effort.
• **Budget 30% of total project time for evaluation.** Testing AI features is more labor-intensive than testing traditional features because you need human judgment to assess quality.
• **Plan for three major prompt revisions.** The first prompt gets you to 60% quality. The second gets you to 80%. The third gets you to 85-90%. Each revision takes longer than the last because the remaining issues are harder.

sections.title: Risk Assessment for AI Initiatives
sections.content:
AI projects carry risks that traditional software projects don't face. Some of these risks are technical, some are organizational, and some are reputational. A PM who identifies and mitigates these risks early earns trust from engineering and leadership alike.

**Technical Risks**

• **Data quality risk** — Claude's output quality is directly tied to the quality of the data you provide. If your knowledge base is outdated, inconsistent, or incomplete, the AI feature will amplify those problems. Assess your data before committing to a timeline.
• **Model behavior drift** — Claude's behavior can change with model updates. A prompt that works perfectly with one model version might produce slightly different results after an update. Mitigation: pin model versions for production features and test new versions before migrating.
• **Latency risk** — Claude API calls take time. If your feature requires sub-second responses and Claude takes 2-3 seconds, you have an architecture problem. Test latency early with production-realistic prompts (not short test prompts).
• **Cost escalation** — API costs are proportional to usage. If your feature goes viral or gets misused, costs can spike unexpectedly. Mitigation: implement rate limiting, usage monitoring, and cost alerts from day one.

**Organizational Risks**

• **Expectation misalignment** — Leadership expects a polished feature by launch. Engineering expects an iterative, improving system. If these expectations aren't aligned, the project will feel like a failure to someone regardless of outcome.
• **Skill gaps** — Prompt engineering is a new skill. Your team may not have someone who's good at it yet. Budget time for learning, or consider bringing in external expertise for the first project.
• **Ownership ambiguity** — Who owns prompt quality? Who monitors output accuracy post-launch? Who decides when to update prompts vs. change the feature design? Define ownership clearly before the project starts.

**Reputational Risks**

• **Hallucination in high-stakes contexts** — If Claude generates incorrect information and it reaches a customer, the trust damage can be severe. The mitigation isn't just technical (better prompts) — it's architectural (human review before any customer-facing output).
• **Bias and fairness** — Claude may produce outputs that reflect biases in its training data. If your feature touches hiring, lending, healthcare, or other sensitive domains, bias testing is mandatory, not optional.
• **Competitive leakage** — Be cautious about what data you send to the Claude API. Customer data, proprietary algorithms, and trade secrets flowing through external APIs requires a data security review.

**The Risk Register Approach**

Maintain a living risk register for every AI project. For each risk, track:

• **Probability** — How likely is this risk to materialize? (High/Medium/Low)
• **Impact** — If it does materialize, how severe is the consequence? (High/Medium/Low)
• **Mitigation** — What specific actions reduce the probability or impact?
• **Owner** — Who is responsible for monitoring this risk?
• **Trigger** — What signals would indicate this risk is materializing?

Review the risk register weekly during active development and biweekly post-launch. Risks evolve as the project progresses — new risks emerge and old risks resolve.

sections.title: Managing AI Development Sprints
sections.content:
AI development doesn't fit neatly into traditional two-week sprints. The iteration cycle is different, the feedback loops are longer, and the definition of "progress" is less clear. Adapting your sprint structure to these realities makes the difference between a team that ships and a team that spins.

**Why Standard Sprints Break Down**

In traditional development, a sprint delivers a set of features that are either done or not done. In AI development, the primary work product is often an improvement in output quality — from 68% to 74% acceptance rate, for example. This creates several problems:

• **Progress is incremental, not binary.** A sprint might deliver a 4-percentage-point improvement in accuracy. That's real progress, but it doesn't feel like a shippable unit of work to stakeholders used to seeing completed features.
• **Debugging is exploratory.** When a traditional feature has a bug, you find the code that's wrong and fix it. When an AI output is bad, you need to analyze the input, the prompt, the context, and the model behavior. This investigation doesn't map to predictable story points.
• **Evaluation is a bottleneck.** Every prompt change needs to be tested against a representative set of inputs. This evaluation step takes time and doesn't parallelize well because it requires human judgment.

**Adapting Your Sprint Structure**

Rather than abandoning sprints, adapt them for AI work:

• **Use "exploration sprints" for new AI features.** The first 1-2 sprints of an AI project should be explicitly labeled as exploration. The output is a feasibility assessment and quality baseline, not shippable code. Set expectations accordingly.
• **Track quality metrics as sprint goals.** Instead of "complete 8 user stories," set goals like "improve acceptance rate from 68% to 75%" or "reduce hallucination rate below 5%." These are measurable and meaningful, even if they feel unfamiliar.
• **Batch prompt changes.** Don't ship prompt changes one at a time. Make several changes, test them together against your evaluation set, and ship the batch that improves overall quality. This is more efficient than evaluating every change individually.
• **Reserve sprint capacity for evaluation.** In a typical AI development sprint, plan for 30-40% of engineering time to go toward evaluating and testing changes, not building them. If your team is spending all their time building and none evaluating, quality will degrade.

**The Prompt Iteration Workflow**

Establish a repeatable workflow for improving AI quality:

• **Collect failure examples** — Gather specific inputs where Claude produced poor outputs. Categorize them by failure type (wrong format, missing information, hallucination, wrong tone).
• **Analyze root causes** — For each failure category, determine whether the issue is prompt-related (fixable by changing instructions), context-related (fixable by providing better input data), or model-related (a fundamental limitation).
• **Propose prompt changes** — Draft specific prompt modifications targeted at the most impactful failure categories.
• **Evaluate against the test set** — Run the new prompt against your full evaluation set, not just the failures. Fixing one failure mode sometimes introduces regressions in other areas.
• **Ship or iterate** — If overall quality improved without significant regressions, ship the change. If regressions appeared, investigate and iterate.

**Communicating Progress to Stakeholders**

Stakeholders need to see progress, but traditional metrics (velocity, stories completed) don't capture AI development progress. Use these alternatives:

• **Quality dashboards** — Show acceptance rate, accuracy, and error rate trends over time. A chart showing steady improvement builds confidence.
• **Before/after examples** — Concrete examples of outputs that improved are more compelling than aggregate metrics. Show a bad output from week 1 and the improved version from week 4.
• **Failure mode burndown** — Track the number of known failure categories and show them decreasing over time. This gives stakeholders a sense of converging quality.

sections.title: Cross-Functional Collaboration
sections.content:
AI features touch more teams than traditional features. Engineering builds the integration, design shapes the user experience, data teams provide the content Claude needs, and legal ensures compliance. The PM sits at the center, orchestrating these groups toward a shared outcome.

**Engineering Collaboration**

Your relationship with engineering on AI projects requires more ongoing communication than typical features:

• **Prompt engineering is a shared responsibility.** The PM defines what the output should look like (format, tone, content). The engineer figures out how to make Claude produce it (prompt structure, context management, API parameters). Neither can do this alone.
• **Establish a prompt review process.** System prompts are product-defining artifacts — they shape user experience as directly as UI design. PMs should review and approve system prompts before they ship, just like they review copy and UI mocks.
• **Define the fallback contract.** What happens when Claude's API is down, slow, or returns an error? The fallback experience is a product decision. Don't leave it to engineering to decide in the moment — specify it in the requirements.

**Design Collaboration**

AI features create new UX challenges that traditional features don't face:

• **Transparency design** — How does the user know something was generated by AI? Subtle labels, different visual treatment, or explicit disclaimers? This is a design and product decision that affects trust.
• **Editing and control** — When Claude generates a draft, how does the user edit it? Can they regenerate? Can they provide feedback? The editing experience often determines whether users adopt the feature.
• **Loading and streaming** — Claude takes time to generate responses. Streaming text, progress indicators, or skeleton screens all handle this differently. Work with design to choose the pattern that fits your feature's context.
• **Error states** — When Claude produces a bad output, what does the user see? A generic error message? A prompt to try again? The error experience is often an afterthought but is critical for AI features.

**Data and Content Team Collaboration**

The quality of Claude's outputs depends heavily on the quality of the context it receives:

• **Knowledge base curation** — If Claude retrieves information from a knowledge base (RAG), that content needs to be accurate, current, and well-structured. Work with content teams to establish a maintenance cadence.
• **Training data for evaluation** — You need representative examples of inputs and expected outputs to evaluate prompt quality. Data teams can help assemble and label these evaluation sets.
• **Data pipeline reliability** — If Claude depends on real-time data feeds (user history, product catalog, etc.), pipeline failures will cause feature failures. Coordinate with data engineering on SLAs and monitoring.

**Legal and Compliance Collaboration**

AI features often trigger legal review requirements that PMs aren't accustomed to:

• **Data processing agreements** — Sending user data to the Claude API may require updated privacy policies or data processing agreements. Start this conversation early — legal review takes time.
• **Output liability** — If Claude generates advice or recommendations, who is liable if the advice is wrong? Legal needs to review customer-facing language around AI-generated content.
• **Regulatory requirements** — Certain industries (financial services, healthcare, education) have specific regulations about automated decision-making. Confirm your feature doesn't trigger compliance requirements before investing in development.
• **Intellectual property** — If Claude generates content based on proprietary data, who owns the output? Clarify IP considerations with legal before launch.

sections.title: Create a Project Plan for an AI Feature Rollout
sections.content: Apply what you've learned about AI project planning by creating a realistic project plan for an AI-powered feature from proof-of-concept through production launch.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM tasked with building a Claude-powered feature that automatically generates personalized onboarding checklists for new enterprise customers. The feature analyzes the customer's industry, company size, purchased product tier, and stated goals (from a sales questionnaire) to generate a customized 30-day onboarding plan with specific milestones and recommended actions.

Your team: 2 backend engineers, 1 frontend engineer, 1 designer, and access to a data analyst. You have 8 weeks until the target launch date.

Create a project plan that includes:

1. **Phase Breakdown** — Divide the 8 weeks into phases (feasibility, prompt development, integration, evaluation, launch). For each phase, specify the duration, key activities, and exit criteria — what must be true before you move to the next phase.
2. **Risk Register** — Identify the top 4 risks (at least one technical, one organizational, and one reputational). For each risk, specify probability, impact, mitigation strategy, owner, and early warning trigger.
3. **Cross-Functional Coordination** — Describe how you'd work with each team (engineering, design, data, legal) during the project. What does each team need from you, and when?
4. **Sprint Structure** — How would you structure sprints for this project? Where would you deviate from standard sprint practices and why?
5. **Stakeholder Communication Plan** — How and when would you update leadership, the customer success team (who will use this feature), and the sales team (who will sell it)?
hints: The feasibility phase should answer: Can Claude generate useful onboarding plans given the input data we have? Test with real customer profiles from your CRM, not hypothetical ones.
A major risk is that the sales questionnaire data may be inconsistent or incomplete — different sales reps fill it out differently. How does this affect Claude's output quality?
Design needs to be involved early to define how the generated checklist is displayed, edited, and shared with the customer. Don't treat design as a polish step at the end.
Legal needs to review any customer-facing AI-generated content — especially if it includes specific recommendations or timelines. Start this conversation in week 1, not week 7.
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A phase breakdown with realistic durations that add up to 8 weeks, where each phase has specific exit criteria (not just 'phase complete') — the feasibility phase should explicitly test with real data and have a go/no-go decision point. (2) A risk register with at least 4 risks covering technical, organizational, and reputational categories. Each risk needs probability, impact, mitigation, owner, and trigger — generic risks like 'project might be late' without specifics are insufficient. (3) Cross-functional coordination that specifies what each team contributes and when — design, legal, and data involvement must start before the integration phase, not after. (4) A sprint structure that acknowledges AI-specific challenges (exploration sprints, evaluation time, prompt iteration cycles) — copy-pasting a standard sprint format without AI adaptations misses the point. (5) A stakeholder communication plan with specific cadences, formats, and content tailored to each group. Deduct points for plans that treat AI development like traditional development, that don't include evaluation time, or that leave legal/compliance review to the last week.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

• Why should you test the hardest cases first during the feasibility phase, not the easiest?
• What's the difference between a traditional sprint goal and an appropriate sprint goal for AI development work?
• Name two risks specific to AI projects that wouldn't appear on a risk register for a traditional software feature.
• Why should legal review happen at the beginning of an AI project rather than at the end?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Managing AI projects successfully requires adapting your planning, execution, and communication practices to account for the unique nature of AI development:

• **Plan in phases, not waterfalls.** AI projects need a feasibility phase with a real go/no-go decision, a prompt development phase with quality targets, and an ongoing iteration phase post-launch. Treat each phase as a distinct planning unit.

• **Budget for uncertainty.** Double your prompt development estimates, allocate 30% of sprint time to evaluation, and plan for at least three major prompt revisions. AI features get better gradually, not all at once.

• **Identify AI-specific risks early.** Data quality, model behavior drift, cost escalation, and expectation misalignment are risks unique to AI projects. Surface them in week 1, not week 7.

• **Adapt your sprints.** Use exploration sprints for early phases, track quality metrics as sprint goals, and reserve capacity for evaluation. Traditional velocity metrics don't capture AI development progress.

• **Start cross-functional coordination immediately.** Design, legal, and data teams all need early involvement in AI projects. Treating them as downstream handoffs creates bottlenecks and compliance surprises late in the project.
